description: Posture-aware trigger suite for git-worktree-workflows. The suite checks whether the skill stays manual-first, routes only on explicit or unmistakable multi-checkout requests, declines adjacent general Git tasks, and keeps repo-specific bootstrap concerns outside the default worktree path.

assertion_definitions.routing_verdict_correct: The response makes the right top-level call: route into git-worktree-workflows only for explicit or high-confidence worktree requests, and decline adjacent Git tasks that do not really need a worktree workflow.
assertion_definitions.manual_first_boundary_explicit: The response treats the skill as manual-first: it either says the request is strong enough to trigger the worktree skill, or explains why the request should stay outside that boundary.
assertion_definitions.stage_routing_correct: For routed requests, the response chooses the smallest sensible route such as Decide, Create, Inspect, Cleanup, or Repair / Recover instead of dumping the entire playbook.
assertion_definitions.negative_boundary_respected: For non-routed requests, the response explains the boundary clearly, including when bootstrap policy, general Git help, or broader advanced-Git work should stay outside this skill.
assertion_definitions.next_step_explicit: The response ends with a concrete next step such as creating the worktree, inspecting existing checkouts, continuing on the current branch, or using a broader Git workflow instead.

cases.name: Trigger on an explicit hotfix worktree request
cases.description: A high-confidence worktree scenario with uncommitted local work and an urgent hotfix.
cases.prompt: A user says: 'I have uncommitted feature work in my current checkout, but I need a clean hotfix from main. Should I use a git worktree for this, and what route applies?' Using git-worktree-workflows, answer in one short paragraph whether this is strong enough to trigger the manual-first skill, what route applies, and the next step. Do not edit files.

manual_first_boundary_explicit.check: The answer should make it clear that this is a strong match for the manual-first worktree skill.

stage_routing_correct.check: The route should focus on Decide plus Create.

next_step_explicit.check: The answer should end with the next step, such as creating the worktree from the integration branch.

cases.name: Trigger on the classic already-checked-out error
cases.description: A first-class worktree debugging request that should route to Inspect before any workaround.
cases.prompt: A user says: 'Git says feature/auth is already checked out in another worktree. Is this a case for the git-worktree-workflows skill, and what route should it take?' Using git-worktree-workflows, answer in one short paragraph whether this should trigger the manual-first skill, what route applies, and the next step. Do not edit files.

manual_first_boundary_explicit.check: The answer should say this is squarely inside the skill boundary.

stage_routing_correct.check: The route should inspect existing worktrees first, then branch or detach only if needed.

next_step_explicit.check: The answer should end with a concrete inspection step such as locating the existing worktree.

cases.name: Trigger on a high-confidence parallel checkout request without naming worktrees
cases.description: The user does not say git worktree explicitly, but the request clearly asks for an isolated parallel review checkout.
cases.prompt: A user says: 'I need to review a teammate's remote branch side by side without stashing my current changes. Is this strong enough to use the worktree skill?' Using git-worktree-workflows, answer in one short paragraph whether this should trigger the manual-first skill, what route applies, and the next step. Do not edit files.

routing_verdict_correct.check: This should still route because the parallel isolated checkout need is explicit even without the word worktree.

manual_first_boundary_explicit.check: The answer should explain why this is a high-confidence enough match for a manual-first skill.

stage_routing_correct.check: The route should remain focused on Decide plus Create for a review checkout.

next_step_explicit.check: The answer should end with the next step, such as fetching the branch and creating the review worktree.

cases.name: Do not trigger for an ordinary branch switch
cases.description: A clean, routine Git task with no multi-checkout need.
cases.prompt: A user says: 'My checkout is clean. Should I switch from main to feature/docs or make a worktree first?' Using git-worktree-workflows, answer in one short paragraph whether this should trigger the skill and why or why not. Do not edit files.

routing_verdict_correct.check: This should not trigger the skill because a normal branch switch is enough.

manual_first_boundary_explicit.check: The answer should explain that this is below the activation threshold for a manual-first worktree skill.

negative_boundary_respected.check: The answer should point to the simpler Git path instead of stretching the skill boundary.

next_step_explicit.check: The answer should end with the simpler next step, such as switching branches directly.

cases.name: Do not trigger for a broader advanced-Git session
cases.description: Worktrees are only incidental in a bigger rebase-oriented task.
cases.prompt: A user says: 'Help me clean up a messy interactive rebase, and I may also need to compare a couple branches while I do it.' Using git-worktree-workflows, answer in one short paragraph whether this should trigger the skill and why or why not. Do not edit files.

routing_verdict_correct.check: This should not trigger git-worktree-workflows as the primary skill.

negative_boundary_respected.check: The answer should explain that broader advanced-Git guidance is the better primary workflow unless worktrees become the main issue.

next_step_explicit.check: The answer should name the more appropriate next step or workflow.

cases.name: Do not treat repo bootstrap as default worktree guidance
cases.description: The user asks about copying environment files and reinstalling dependencies after creating a second checkout.
cases.prompt: A user says: 'I already made a second checkout. How should I copy .env, link .venv, and reinstall dependencies there?' Using git-worktree-workflows, answer in one short paragraph whether this should trigger the skill and why or why not. Do not edit files.

routing_verdict_correct.check: This should not route as a default worktree-skill task.

negative_boundary_respected.check: The answer should explain that bootstrap overlays are repo-specific conventions, not generic worktree behavior.

next_step_explicit.check: The answer should point to project docs or a repo-specific setup workflow.

scoring.notes: Use this suite as a manual trigger-boundary review. The goal is to confirm that the skill stays manual-first, routes only on clear worktree signals, and declines adjacent Git tasks that do not really need a worktree workflow.

runner_instructions.setup: Ensure the canonical skill package is discoverable. These prompts are classification-only and should be run without editing files.
runner_instructions.execution: Run each prompt in a session where the skill is available. Capture whether the skill would route, what route it picks, and what next step it recommends.
runner_instructions.evaluation: Score whether the response respected the manual-first boundary, picked the smallest sensible route, and stayed out of repo-specific bootstrap advice.
runner_instructions.environment: No file edits are required for these prompts. They are meant for posture and routing review.
